text.title: Story Map Structure: Backbone, Walking Skeleton, and Release Slices
text.summary: Defines the three structural layers of a user story map and the rules that govern each.

rule.statement: Use verb phrases for backbone activities; arrange left-to-right in user journey order; limit to 5-10 items.
rule.rationale: Noun-only labels ("Project") lose the action context the team needs. More than 10 activities collapses into a Gantt chart and loses strategic clarity.

example.note: E-commerce backbone: Browse Products → View Product → Add to Cart → Checkout → Receive Order
Backbone labelled "Products, Cart, Payment, Order" — nouns only, no journey verb.

rule.statement: Place user tasks vertically under each backbone activity, ordered top-to-bottom by priority within that column. Every task must be user-observable, not an implementation step.
rule.rationale: Vertical position encodes priority within an activity. "Authenticate user" is an implementation step; "Sign in with email" is user-observable.

rule.statement: Include at least one error or recovery task per backbone column.
rule.rationale: Happy-path-only maps under-deliver; error flows are discovered after launch rather than before.

rule.statement: Identify exactly one task per backbone activity for the walking skeleton. The skeleton must span every column — gaps mean the journey cannot be completed end-to-end.
rule.rationale: A skeleton missing one column produces an MVP that fails at that step. Completeness is non-negotiable.

example.note: Walking skeleton for e-commerce: See product list | See details | Add item | Enter payment | Get confirmation — one task per activity, all five columns covered.

antipattern.description: Walking skeleton silently dropped because "nothing felt minimal" for one activity.
antipattern.reason: Every activity exists because the user must complete it. If no task is minimal there, the activity itself is wrong.

rule.statement: Draw horizontal lines grouping tasks into shippable releases. Each slice must deliver standalone user value — it is not just a list of features.
rule.rationale: A slice that depends on a later slice for value is not a release; it is a phase. Phases are not shippable.

rule.statement: Limit to 3-5 release slices. More than 5 destroys release thinking and turns the map into a roadmap.
rule.rationale: Beyond 5 slices the distinction between releases blurs and planning reverts to feature-list mode.

example.note: Release 1 for e-commerce: Filter by category | See images | Update quantity | Enter shipping | Email receipt — one thin horizontal slice per activity, all columns covered.
